title: Understanding U-values and what they mean for your home
subtitle: How thermal transmittance translates into heat loss and money
excerpt: A U-value tells you how fast heat moves through a wall, floor, or roof. Lower is better. This guide explains what the numbers mean, how they combine with area and temperature difference, and how to use them to prioritise retrofit decisions.
sourceNotes: gov.uk Building Regulations Part L 2022 Approved Document, Appendix A U-value reference table. BRE Report BR 443 Conventions for U-value Calculations (2006). Energy Saving Trust insulation guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: Thermal transmittance. The watts of heat that flow through one square metre of building fabric per degree of temperature difference (W/m²·K). Lower is better; uninsulated solid brick is ~2.1, current Part L new-build wall target is 0.18.
Thermal resistance. The inverse of U-value (m²·K/W). Higher is better. Common on US insulation packaging; UK packaging usually quotes lambda + depth instead.

Thermal conductivity (W/m·K). The intrinsic property of an insulation material — mineral wool 0.035–0.044, PIR boards 0.022–0.024, sheep wool 0.038–0.040. Combined with depth, gives the achieved U-value.

A localised gap or break in the insulation envelope where heat short-circuits the resistance — a steel lintel through a brick wall, a window reveal, a junction between insulated wall and uninsulated floor.

content.text: Heat moves through a wall at a rate proportional to three things: the area of the wall, the temperature difference between inside and outside, and a property of the wall's construction called its
U-value
. The formula is: heat loss (W) = U × area (m²) × ΔT (°C). A lower U-value means slower heat transfer; adding insulation reduces U.
What the numbers mean
Uninsulated solid brick wall (Victorian): ~2.1 W/m²·K
Cavity wall, pre-1995, unfilled: ~1.5 W/m²·K
Cavity wall with blown mineral wool fill: ~0.4-0.5 W/m²·K
Loft, 100 mm mineral wool: ~0.5 W/m²·K
Loft, 270 mm mineral wool (current best practice): ~0.13 W/m²·K
Double glazing (1990s argon gap): ~2.8 W/m²·K
Double glazing (modern low-e argon): ~1.4-1.6 W/m²·K
Triple glazing (modern): ~0.8-1.0 W/m²·K
Worked example

A 1930s Leeds semi has a north-facing rear wall 7.2 m wide × 5.4 m tall = 39 m² (minus 4 m² for a window, so 35 m² of wall). It is an unfilled cavity wall, U-value 1.5 W/m²·K. The design temperature difference (inside 20°C minus UK outside design temperature of -3°C) is 23°C.
Heat loss through that wall: 1.5 × 35 × 23 = 1,207 W. Running at full boiler output for 2500 heating hours per year: 1.207 kW × 2500 h = 3,018 kWh. At 6p/kWh gas, that is just from that one wall.
After cavity fill (U = 0.45 W/m²·K): 0.45 × 35 × 23 = 362 W. Annual heat: 0.362 × 2500 = 905 kWh = . Saving: from one wall, at a cavity fill cost of ~ installed.
Relating U-value to lambda and insulation depth
The relationship between insulation depth and U-value improvement goes through
lambda value
and
R-value

. R = depth / lambda. Total R = sum of all layers. U = 1 / total R. For mineral wool at lambda 0.040: 100 mm gives R = 2.5 m²·K/W. 270 mm gives R = 6.75 m²·K/W, corresponding to U = 0.13 (not accounting for surface resistances and joist bridging, which are small corrections).
Thermal bridges
The U-value calculation assumes a homogeneous, uninterrupted layer. In practice,
thermal bridges

window reveals, steel lintels, joist ends penetrating the insulation line, bypass the calculation. A wall with excellent main-area U-value but uninsulated reveals can still have significant total heat loss at the bridging paths. The Part L approved document includes correction factors for common bridging geometries.
How to use U-values to prioritise

Rank retrofits by: (current U − improved U) × area. The largest improvement on the largest surface area delivers the greatest saving. For most UK homes, this ranking comes out roughly as: loft (largest area, largest U-value improvement) → cavity walls → solid walls → floor → windows. Draught-proofing is not a U-value improvement but is usually first in priority because payback is immediate.
Where to find U-values for existing construction

Your EPC report includes the assessed U-values for each element of your building fabric. BRE Report BR 443 (free download from BRE Group) gives reference U-values for over 200 construction types. If the house has already been partially retrofitted, the installer should have provided a certificate with the measured or calculated post-retrofit U-value.
